metadata.title: Research Combination Chemotherapy Design Logic with Claude: Why MOPP and R-CHOP Work
metadata.color_semantics: teardown: INK #2A1A0E body text; CRIMSON #C8102E key finding/vulnerability; SLATE #545454 structure/neutral

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'NikBearBrownOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Hitting harder is not the logic. Hitting differently is.

The slogan chemotherapy kills fast-dividing cells is mostly true and partly false. It cannot explain why BEP cures testicular cancer or why vincristine appears in so many regimens despite being one of the weakest individual agents. There is a design logic behind combination chemotherapy — three rules that explain the regimen's efficacy — and it is not about hitting harder.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — problem card: the false slogan vs the three design rules; vincristine paradox

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The slogan chemotherapy kills fast-dividing cells is mostly true and partly false. It cannot explain why BEP cures testicular cancer or why vincristine appears

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to apply the three classical rules to R-CHOP and explain what each drug contributes.

props.command:
claude "The three classical rules for combination chemo:
(1) different mechanisms, (2) non-overlapping
dose-limiting toxicities, (3) demonstrated single-
agent activity. Apply these rules to R-CHOP
(rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin,
vincristine, prednisone). For each drug: mechanism
class, dose-limiting toxicity, and what would happen
if it were replaced by a drug with the same mechanism
as another drug already in the regimen."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — Python script reading Claude response
beats.narration_text: Read the matrix. Rituximab: anti-CD20 antibody, infusion reaction dose-limiting. Cyclophosphamide: alkylating agent, myelosuppression. Doxorubicin: topoisomerase II inhibitor, cumulative cardiotoxicity. Vincristine: microtubule inhibitor, peripheral neuropathy — minimal myelosuppression, which is exactly why it is here. Prednisone: glucocorticoid, immunosuppression and hyperglycemia. Five drugs, five different toxicity organs.

props.code:
# rchop_matrix.py -- Claude Code output
RCHOP = [
  {"drug": "R  Rituximab",
   "mech": "Anti-CD20 mAb",
   "tox":  "Infusion reaction",
   "organ": "Immune/vascular"},
  {"drug": "C  Cyclophosphamide",
   "mech": "Alkylating agent (DNA cross-link)",
   "tox":  "Myelosuppression (dose-limiting)",
   "organ": "Bone marrow"},
  {"drug": "H  Doxorubicin",
   "mech": "Topo II inhibitor / intercalator",
   "tox":  "Cardiotoxicity (cumul. >450 mg/m2)",
   "organ": "Heart"},
  {"drug": "O  Vincristine",
   "mech": "Microtubule inhibitor (tubulin bind)",
   "tox":  "Peripheral neuropathy — NOT marrow",
   "organ": "Peripheral nervous system"},
  {"drug": "P  Prednisone",
   "mech": "Glucocorticoid (transcription)",
   "tox":  "Hyperglycemia / immunosuppression",
   "organ": "Metabolic"},
]
# Rule check: no two drugs share dose-limiting organ

beats.narration_text: The matrix. Five drugs, two attributes — mechanism and dose-limiting toxicity. Color-coded by toxicity organ. No two drugs share the same organ. That is not a coincidence — it is the design rule made visible. Each drug can be pushed to its own maximum without the others' toxicities compounding.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: B04_ChemoMatrix — five-drug matrix with mechanism and toxicity columns, color-coded by organ system

beats.narration_text: Add the carboplatin dosing exception. Ask Claude why it is dosed by AUC rather than body surface area, and what that reveals about the therapeutic window concept made quantitative.

props.command:
claude "Add the carboplatin dosing exception:
why is carboplatin dosed by AUC using the Calvert
formula rather than body surface area? What does
this reveal about the therapeutic window concept
made quantitative? How does renal function enter
the Calvert formula and why does that matter?"

beats.narration_text: The Calvert formula: dose in milligrams equals AUC times GFR plus twenty-five. Carboplatin is cleared entirely by the kidneys. Body surface area does not predict renal clearance — GFR does. The formula makes the therapeutic window explicit and quantitative: AUC of five to six for most combinations, AUC of seven for single-agent high-dose. The number encodes the toxicity budget.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — Calvert formula annotated; AUC as quantitative therapeutic window; renal function connection

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The Calvert formula: dose in milligrams equals AUC times GFR plus twenty-five. Carboplatin is cleared entirely by the kidneys. Body surface area does not predic

beats.narration_text: Combination chemotherapy is not about hitting harder. It is about hitting differently so that resistance to one mechanism does not confer resistance to all others, and so that each drug can be pushed to its own maximum without the others' toxicities compounding. The design logic behind the regimen is the mechanism of its efficacy.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — summary card: different mechanisms + non-overlapping toxicity = the design logic is the efficacy

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Combination chemotherapy is not about hitting harder. It is about hitting differently so that resistance to one mechanism does not confer resistance to all othe

beats.narration_text: When you encounter a chemotherapy regimen, apply the three-rule test: different mechanisms, non-overlapping toxicities, single-agent activity. Then check whether the dose-limiting toxicity for each drug is truly distinct. If two drugs share the same organ, ask why — that is either a deliberate design exception or a reason to look for a better combination.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE — next steps: three-rule checklist for any regimen; shared-organ exception flag
